data: Executive Lobbying and the Quest for Autonomy

Brazil is Starlink's second-largest global market, with 250,000 active users and 85% annual growth in 2025. Despite strong urban penetration, only 22% of Brazilians meet 'meaningful connectivity' standards.
5 providers authorized · 250,000 Starlink subscribers · R$102,677 licensing fee until 2027
R$102,677 in licensing fees for rights worth billions — less than R$5 per satellite per year
The authorization was actively engineered by the Bolsonaro government, which lobbied the regulator and met directly with SpaceX weeks before the regulatory framework was active.
Starlink operates via Starlink Brazil Holding Ltda. and holds an exploitation right for spectrum and a retail broadband license.
While five providers are authorized on paper, the market is in practice a near-monopoly where Starlink is the only operational consumer LEO player at scale.
The framework initially lacked data localization requirements, space sustainability rules, and mechanisms for state control during geopolitical disputes.

Brazil is Starlink's second-largest global market, with 250,000 active users and 85% annual growth in 2025. Despite 85% urban internet penetration, only 22% of Brazilians meet 'meaningful connectivity' standards, exposing a sharp class divide: 83% of Class A households are meaningfully connected versus just 1% of Class DE. The government granted Starlink exploitation rights for R$102,677 until 2027 — less than R$5 per satellite per year — raising serious questions about regulatory capture. The 2024 X/Twitter ban crisis, in which Musk threatened to defy Brazilian court orders, prompted the reactive authorization of SpaceSail as a geopolitical hedge. Meanwhile, 50 Starlink terminals were seized from illegal mining operations in Yanomami indigenous territory, illustrating the governance vacuum around infrastructure misuse.

RGSat (Resolution 748/2021) — authorization process heavily influenced by political lobbying from the Bolsonaro government; a ten-day public comment window applied to exploitation rights applications, very short by comparative regulatory standards.
1 of 7 governance indicators met. A ten-day public comment window existed but was criticized as too brief; no cybersecurity, QoS, or data residency obligations were included.
Licensing fee of R$102,677 for exploitation rights until 2027 — less than R$5 per satellite per year
Authorization process influenced by political lobbying; 8 formal government meetings with Starlink in one year
50 Starlink terminals seized from illegal mining and deforestation operations in Yanomami territory
SpaceSail (China) authorized as reactive geopolitical hedge — not through structured sovereignty policy
Meaningful connectivity gap: 83% Class A vs 1% Class DE — LEO growth not reaching the most underserved
Multi-constellation mandate to reduce single-provider dependency
Fair contribution mechanism to Universal Service Funds
Meaningful connectivity standards linked to licensing conditions
Who controls satellite internet? We’re tracking who profits from connectivity in space and who gets left behind.

LEO satellite governance, Starlink regulation, digital sovereignty, satellite internet policy, orbital commons, satellite market concentration, connectivity divide, spectrum equity, ITU WRC-27, satellite licensing transparency
